explicit_text: I first learned about the attacks on the World Trade Center when my wife called me to turn on the television. I watched live footage of the first tower being struck and then the second tower shortly after. It was shocking to witness, and it took some time to come to grips with the reality of what was happening in New York City that day.

explicit_text: At the time of the attacks, I was focused on understanding the situation from the National Park Service perspective. Since none of our sites were directly affected, we were not immediately mobilized but were trying to gather accurate information amidst the confusion. Our Park Police were involved in supporting traffic and security operations, especially around the White House, but the main challenge was managing communication and response within the agency.

explicit_text: My experience as superintendent of Everglades National Park during Hurricane Andrew shaped how I approached the 9/11 attacks. Hurricane Andrew was a significant national disaster localized to that area, and it taught me the importance of slowing down to gather accurate information rather than rushing into action. That experience helped me advise on the response to the 9/11 events within the National Park Service.

This team had experience with large-scale incidents like the Yellowstone fires and was capable of managing national-level emergencies.
